insights: All five incident categories—Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Software, and Network—have exactly 100 incidents each (20.00% share), indicating zero distributional discrepancy and perfect balance across categories.
All five assignees handle exactly 100 incidents each, with Gini coefficient = 0.0000 and max/min ratio = 1.00, confirming no workload imbalance or assignment skew among personnel.
All five geographic locations—Australia, Canada, India, UK, and United States—each account for exactly 100 incidents, eliminating location-based concentration or service coverage disparity.

Cross-tabulation analysis reveals no meaningful specialization: each assignee handles all five categories, all five locations, and all four priority levels, with dominant category shares ranging narrowly from 23% to 27%—only marginally above the uniform baseline of 20%.

Temporal distribution shows low inequality (monthly Gini = 0.0932; quarterly Gini = 0.1688), with the highest quarterly variation driven by truncated data in Q1 2024 (only 34 incidents across one month), not systemic seasonality or operational instability.

The structural 1:1 mapping between category and assignment_group is fully reflected in the data—each assignment group also has exactly 100 incidents—reinforcing system-wide consistency in routing logic and eliminating category-to-group misalignment as a source of imbalance.
summary: The user's goal was to identify discrepancies and imbalances in incident distribution across categories, and the analysis conclusively shows none exist: all five categories, five assignees, and five locations have perfectly uniform incident counts of 100 each (Gini = 0.0000). Cross-tabulations further confirm no hidden concentration—assignees are broadly distributed across all categories, locations, and priority levels, with only negligible deviations from uniformity (e.g., dominant category shares at 23–27%). Temporal patterns also exhibit low inequality, with the sole higher quarterly variation attributable to incomplete data coverage in early 2024—not an operational imbalance. This dataset reflects an exceptionally well-balanced assignment system, suggesting that governance, routing rules, and workload distribution mechanisms are functioning as designed; the next focus should be validating whether this balance persists under increased volume or during incident surges.
